teamObjective.explanation: Partner P2 has only 2 cards remaining and is near finishing. By leading the weakest possible card, I minimize the strength opponents must spend to beat it and give P2 the best chance to take control of the trick and potentially finish the hand.

partnerBelief.summary: Partner P2 likely has a small but possibly strong hand near finish. With 2 cards left, P2 may have conserved trumps or high cards to win a trick, but could also be stuck with unplayable cards. P2 probably wants me to lead low so they can attempt to seize the lead.

opponentBelief.summary: Opponents P1 and P3 each hold 3 cards and have revealed nothing. Given an unplayed deck, it is probable that at least one opponent can beat a low diamond single with a higher non-trump or a trump. They will try to prevent P2 from gaining lead and finishing.

actionRationale.primaryReason: Leading the 3♦, my lowest card, is the safest way to open the trick. It conserves my slightly stronger diamonds, forces opponents to reveal information, and gives my near-finish partner the most favorable position to win the trick with a trump or high card.

whyNotAlternatives.reason: The 6♦ is my highest non-trump but is still weak; any opponent with a 7 or trump can beat it easily. Playing it now wastes my best card and does not materially increase the chance of helping partner finish.
The 5♦ is intermediate: stronger than the 3 but still a low non-trump. It is more likely to be beaten by ordinary opponent cards than the 3, yet no more likely to let partner win.
The 4♦ is only marginally stronger than the 3 and offers no strategic advantage over leading the absolute lowest card.

risks: Any opponent with a trump (rank 7) or higher non-trump can immediately take the lead.
Partner P2 may not have a card that can beat opponents, causing us to lose initiative.
If opponents gain lead, they may play combinations that my weak diamond hand cannot match.
riskAssessment.mitigation: Leading the minimum-strength card keeps my best resources in reserve, avoids overcommitting, and maximizes the chance that partner can win with a conserved strong card. Even if opponents take this trick, I have not weakened my position further.

notes: Beliefs remain probabilistic because no cards have been played. The partner-near-finish signal comes from public hand counts, and the only public event is the new_game event.
